title: React Best Practices – Writing Clean & Scalable Code

meta.title: Professional React Best Practices – Clean Code & Patterns (2026)
meta.description: Master the industry standards for React development. Learn about component composition, custom hooks, performance patterns, and maintainable project structures.

content.value: Writing React code that works is easy; writing React code that is maintainable, testable, and performant in a team environment is the real challenge. In 2026, best practices revolve around functional programming, declarative UI, and strict separation of concerns.
Avoid 'God Components'—huge files that handle everything. Break UI into small, reusable pieces. If a component exceeds 150 lines or handles more than one responsibility, it's time to split it.

value: **Single Responsibility**: One component should do one thing (e.g., a Button, a UserCard, or a Form).
**Composition over Inheritance**: Use the `children` prop to create flexible layouts instead of complex prop configurations.
**Extract Logic**: Move complex business logic out of JSX and into Custom Hooks.

content.value: Mismanaged state is the #1 cause of bugs and performance issues. Keep state as local as possible and only lift it up when absolutely necessary.
Custom hooks are the best way to share logic between components. If you find yourself writing the same `useEffect` or `useState` logic in two places, abstract it.

value: **Optional Chaining**: Use `user?.profile?.name` to avoid 'cannot read property of undefined' crashes.
**Prop Types / TypeScript**: Always define the shape of your props to catch bugs at compile-time.
**Sanitize Data**: Never use `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` with unsanitized user input to prevent XSS attacks.

faq.question: Is it okay to use 'index' as a key in a list?
faq.answer: Only if the list is static and never changes. If you add, remove, or sort items, using the index will cause rendering bugs. Always use a unique ID from your data.

faq.question: Should I use functional components exclusively?
faq.answer: Yes, unless you need an Error Boundary, which currently requires a Class Component. Functional components with Hooks are the modern standard.

quiz.question: What is the primary benefit of 'Composition' in React?
options: It makes the code run faster
It allows components to be more flexible and reusable by nesting
It replaces the need for state

quiz.question: Why should you use functional updates like 'setCount(c => c + 1)'?
options: It is less code to write
It ensures you are working with the most current state value
It prevents the component from rendering
